title: Write the brief, then write the prompt

spec.title: Write the brief, then write the prompt
spec.notes_for_generator: Forces learner to define success criteria before prompting. The key insight: most poor prompts fail because the prompter hasn't defined what good looks like. Ask them to write their success criteria BEFORE they write the prompt, then evaluate whether the output meets those criteria. Hinted: suggest defining success criteria first without explaining exactly how.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 12: Write the brief before the prompt

Most prompts fail before they're written. You haven't decided what good looks like yet — so how would you know if you got it?

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
🗂️ STRUCTURING | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 12–18 min

THE BRIEF:
You need to use a chat-based LLM to document a process you know well — a recurring task from your working life. It could be how your team runs a weekly meeting, how a project gets kicked off, how you handle inbound requests, anything with steps and a purpose. Before you write a single word to the AI, you have one thing to do first.

YOUR REPS:
1. Before opening your chosen LLM, write down three things your finished process document must do to be considered good. Not vague things like "clear" or "useful" — specific things. Who needs to follow it? What should they be able to do without asking a follow-up question? What would make it unusable? Write these down somewhere, then build your prompt around them.

2. Now send your prompt and read what comes back against the three criteria you wrote. Not against your gut feeling — against the list. Where does the output fall short? Pick the single biggest gap and write a follow-up prompt that addresses only that gap. Be surgical.

3. Take the improved output and pressure-test it: ask the AI to play the role of someone following this process for the first time. Where do they get confused? Where are the gaps? Use what comes back to decide whether the document is actually done or needs another pass.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• Someone unfamiliar with the process could follow it start to finish without needing to ask a clarifying question
• Every step includes enough context to explain *why* it exists, not just *what* to do
• The final document matches the specific criteria you wrote before you started prompting — not just your general sense that it looks right

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Defining success before you start is what separates a prompt from a brief. When you write the brief first, your prompt becomes more specific, your evaluation becomes honest, and your follow-up prompts target real gaps instead of vague dissatisfaction. This is why professional writers, designers, and strategists write acceptance criteria before they start the work — it changes the quality of the input and the rigour of the review. It transfers to any LLM task: summarisation, analysis, drafting. Define the bar. Then see if you cleared it.

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
